PR [#598](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/598) is green — all 14 checks passing, merge state CLEAN. It's ready for your squash-merge (I'm permission-blocked from merging my own PR to protected `main`; say the word if you want me to do it instead).

**Final state of the v1.1 planning:**

- **Plan**: 6 weeks (2026-07-04 → 2026-08-15) in `docs/progress.md` §Cycle plan, on PR #598 with the CLAUDE.md §13 headline. W1–3 are subscription-driven (Snowflake days, Azure ~07-25), W4–6 run the roadmap's recommended sequence cloud-independently.
- **W1 (due 07-11)**: Snowflake-live trio #194/#195/#587 day 1 → retire the leg #588 → **PATs #461 pulled forward per your call** (exit: 1 admin + 1 member PAT minted) → #583.
- **W2**: OTel logs #524 + OTLP endpoint #589, `SecretStore.delete` #372, dry-run depth #532, MCP NL check #584 on the new PATs.
- **W3**: final live E2E, #492 decision, Azure teardown #590 + local-first posture #591.
- **W4–6**: `schema_drift` #592 (+#427–#429) → `anomaly` #593 + trend view #594 + #568 → scale-aware execution #595, perf #327/#323, G-d design doc #596, hardening #563/#573, retro + `v1.1.0` tag.
- **GitHub**: milestones `v1.1 Week 1..6`, epic #597, 10 new issues (#587–#596), 17 re-milestoned, all 27 on the **DataQ Roadmap** project with the new `v1.1 week` field, new `ops` label.

One CI note: the frontend flake #573 fired once on the rerun-before-last (docs-only diff, known Popconfirm timeout) — I logged the datapoint on the issue; its fix is scheduled in W6.

You can merge the PR once CI is green. 
Can you check one thing in the meantime - azure mention 1 year trial subcription with 30 days free with credits [of which we already spent 1 week], so after the 30 days are over how will it affect us?
CI is already green (14/14, state CLEAN), so merging now, then I'll dig into the Azure trial question.

## Option 2: Sign up for a free Azure account
You can create an [Azure account for free](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/purchase-options/azure-account?cid=msft_learn) and receive 12 months of popular services for free and a $200 credit to explore Azure for 30 days.
## Option 3: Sign up for a pay-as-you-go account
You can also create a [pay-as-you-go Azure account](https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/purchase-options/azure-account?cid=msft_learn). This option includes monthly amounts of select services for free, and charges you for what you use beyond the free limits. There is no upfront commitment and you can cancel anytime.
## Option 4: Use a corporate account
If you are using Azure at work, talk to your company's cloud administrator to get your Azure credentials and then sign in to your account with the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com).","contentUrl":"https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/azure/create-azure-account"},{"title":"Try Azure SQL Managed Instance for free","content":"# Try Azure SQL Managed Instance for free

Additionally, the following limitations apply:
1. The offer is valid for 12 months from the day you activate the offer. You can apply it to only one instance at a time, per subscription.
2. After 12 months, the free SQL managed instance stops. If you don't upgrade the instance to a paid version within 30 days, the instance and all databases are deleted and no longer recoverable.
3. The following capabilities aren't supported: [Zone redundancy](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/managed-instance/high-availability-sla-local-zone-redundancy?view=azuresql#zone-redundant-availability), [Failover groups](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/managed-instance/failover-group-sql-mi?view=azuresql), [Long-term backup retention](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/database/long-term-retention-overview?view=azuresql), modifying the backup storage redundancy.

5. The free offer is available in all [regions that support Azure SQL Managed Instance](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-sql/managed-instance/region-availability?view=azuresql).
6. If you delete a free SQL managed instance, you delete all its databases and can't restore them.
### Monthly vCore limits
The monthly free limits include 720 vCore hours of compute. Your free month of credits starts when you create your instance and renews on the same day of the following month.
When you reach the monthly free vCore limit, the instance stops with a status of `Stopped - Insufficient credit`. A banner on the **Overview** page of your instance in the Azure portal gives you the option to create a new paid instance. content.text:
PR #598 is merged and `main` is up to date. Now the Azure question — I verified the terms against Microsoft's current docs so this isn't from memory. Short version: **day 30 is only a cliff if you don't upgrade; the "1 year" part only kicks in if you do.**

## How the offer actually works

The free account is two separate things:

1. **$200 credit, first 30 days only.** Everything you run is burned against it. Unused credit is lost at day 30 — it never carries past that date, even if you upgrade.
2. **12 months of limited free service quantities** (+ some always-free services). This is the "1 year" — but it only continues **if you upgrade to pay-as-you-go**. Upgrading requires adding a card; you're then charged only for usage *beyond* the free quantities.

**If you do nothing at day 30:** the subscription is **disabled** — frontend, api, worker, Redis, Postgres, ADF polling all stop at once. Resources aren't deleted immediately (there's a grace window where upgrading re-enables them), but the app is effectively dead. One important survivor: **the Entra tenant is not part of the subscription** — the demo users and app registrations keep working, so Azure-AD login for local dev survives even a disabled subscription.

## What DataQ would cost on pay-as-you-go

| DataQ piece | After upgrade |
|---|---|
| Postgres (`dataq-pg-wus3-*`, shared app+harness) | **Free for 12 months** if it's B1MS with ≤32GB — 750 h/month covers 24/7. This is the big one; worth confirming the SKU |
| Container Apps ×4 (frontend, api, worker, redis) | Always-free grant: 180K vCPU-s + 360K GiB-s + 2M requests/month per subscription. A month is ~2.6M seconds, so our always-on worker+redis exceed the grant → the main real cost. Idle-rate billing helps (a quiet worker between beats bills reduced); ballpark **$10–40/month**, less if api scales to zero and sizes are trimmed |
| App Insights / Log Analytics | First 5GB/month ingestion free — our volume is likely within it |
| Key Vault, ADLS, ADF (harness) | Pennies; ADF is per-activity-run — pause the harness triggers if not demoing |

## What this means for the v1.1 plan

The W1–3 sequencing treated ~07-25 as a hard stop. Upgrading changes the calculus:

- **If you upgrade (~$10–40/month):** the Azure deadline disappears. Prod stays live through the whole cycle — which means live E2E for the schema_drift/anomaly/scale weeks too — and W3's #590 becomes a **cost-trim pass** (scale-to-zero, pause harness ADF, prune ingestion) instead of `terraform destroy`. Bonus: upgrading lifts the trial quota caps (the 1-env/1-PG-server limit that forced the shared-server design in ADR 0024). If you go this way: set a **Cost Management budget + alert** (say $30/month) immediately — PAYG has no spend cap.
- **If you don't:** the current plan is exactly right — everything cloud-verified by end of W3, deliberate teardown before the disable hits, and note that whatever's left of the $200 evaporates at day 30 regardless (an argument for *spending* it on the #587 volume baseline and W2 live verification rather than conserving it).

My take: the Snowflake urgency is unchanged either way (separate subscription, days left). For Azure, if a small monthly bill is acceptable, upgrading before day 30 is clearly better value than racing the teardown — but it's a real card-on-file decision, so it's yours. If you decide, I'll adjust #590/#591 and the W3 milestone accordingly.

content.text:
I now have the full picture from the actual Terraform (app stack + harness). One caveat up front: the pricing page hides exact numbers behind JavaScript, so I'm using the standard US-region ACA consumption list rates (≈$63/month per always-active vCPU, ≈$7.90/month per always-idle vCPU, ≈$7.90/month per GiB — idle discount applies to CPU only, memory bills the same either way). Also note: **the local `az` CLI is logged in as demo user Olivia** (leftover from the go-live workout) with Reader on one resource only, so I couldn't pull your real meter data — these are computed from the deployed sizes.

## What's actually deployed (from Terraform)

**App stack — all `min_replicas ≥ 1`, so always allocated:**

| App | Size | Always-on |
|---|---|---|
| `dataq-app-worker` (Celery + beat) | 1.0 vCPU / 2 GiB | yes (beat can't scale to zero) |
| `dataq-app-api` | 0.5 / 1 GiB (max 3) | yes |
| `dataq-app-redis` | 0.5 / 1 GiB | yes |
| `dataq-app-frontend` | 0.25 / 0.5 GiB (max 2) | yes |
| migrate job | 0.5 / 1 GiB | per-run, negligible |

Total: **2.25 vCPU / 4.5 GiB allocated 24/7.**

**Harness adds — this is the surprise:**

| App | Size | Always-on |
|---|---|---|
| Airflow scheduler+webserver | 2.0 vCPU / 4 GiB | yes |
| Airflow workers | 2.0 / 4 GiB **× min 2** | yes — **4.0 vCPU / 8 GiB** |
| harness redis | 0.5 / 1 GiB | yes |
| mockdata cron jobs, ADF (2 triggers), Event Grid, ADLS | — | pennies |

Total: **+6.5 vCPU / +13 GiB always allocated — the harness is ~3× the app's compute.**

## Monthly cost estimate (pay-as-you-go, after free grants)

| Scenario | All-idle (best) | Realistic† | All-active (worst) |
|---|---|---|---|
| App only (harness off) | ~$50 | **~$100–140** | ~$175 |
| Harness only | ~$155 | **~$350–450** | ~$515 |
| **Total, harness on** | ~$205 | **~$450–560** | ~$690 |

†"Realistic" assumes the Celery worker, both redises, the Airflow scheduler and its 2 workers bill *active* (constant broker polling/heartbeats usually exceed the 1,000 bytes/s idle threshold), while api/frontend/webserver sit idle. The idle-vs-active classification is the single biggest uncertainty — only the real bill settles it.

Everything else is roughly free: **Postgres is $0** (`B_Standard_B1ms` + 32 GB is exactly the 12-month free quantity, 750 h/month), Log Analytics likely under the 5 GB free, ADF ~$2–5, KV/Event Grid/storage/bandwidth ~$1–2.

## The urgent implication

If the harness has been fully up since deploy, burn is plausibly **$15–20/day — meaning the $200 credit could run out in days, well before the 30-day mark** (services get disabled at credit exhaustion, not at day 30). I'd check the real balance today: portal → Subscriptions → your Free Trial → credit remaining. If you `az login` as the owner account, I can pull Cost Management month-to-date and replace these estimates with actuals.

## The levers, in order of impact

1. **Scale the harness to zero when not demoing** (Airflow workers min 2→0, scheduler/webserver min 1→0, harness redis off): saves ~$350–450/month — by far the biggest lever, and reversible in one `az containerapp update` / TF variable per app.
2. **Trim the app**: api min 1→0 (cold starts behind the nginx proxy are the tradeoff), worker 1.0→0.5 vCPU (keep 2 GiB for GX/pandas headroom): app drops to roughly **$50–75/month** — that's the realistic steady-state bill if you upgrade to PAYG and keep prod alive through the cycle.
3. Requests never matter here (2M/month free, health probes don't count).

So the upgrade decision from earlier now has real numbers: **harness-off + trimmed app ≈ $50–75/month** to keep prod alive past day 30; harness-on continuously is ~$500/month and isn't worth it — wake it for demo/E2E windows only. Want me to fold a "scale-to-zero harness + app trim" task into #590/#591?
I logged in using royarijit04, can you ncheck once more

content.text:
Full actuals now, from Cost Management (your billing currency is CAD). The picture is sharp:

## Real burn — CAD $21.69/day average (July 1–3), $144.80 consumed since the June 27 deploy

| Resource | CAD/day | Share |
|---|---|---|
| `dataq-harness-airflow` (scheduler+webserver, 2 vCPU/4Gi) | 8.99 | ← biggest single line |
| `dataq-harness-airflow-worker` (2× 2 vCPU/4Gi) | 6.92 | |
| `dataq-harness-redis` + `airflow-trigger` + ADF | 1.50 | |
| **Harness subtotal** | **~17.41** | **80%** |
| `dataq-app-worker` / `api` / `redis` / `frontend` | 4.04 | 19% |
| ACR (Basic, base fee) | 0.24 | |
| Postgres B1ms, Key Vault, logs, storage, mockdata jobs, alerts | **0.00** | free tiers doing their job |

So monthly: **harness ON ≈ CAD $660/mo (~USD $480)** · **harness OFF ≈ CAD $130/mo (~USD $95)** — my Terraform-based estimate held up, and the split answers your question directly: **the harness is 80% of the bill; the app itself is ~$4/day.**

## The runway problem

The Canada-localized free credit is normally **CAD $260** (the "$200" USD grant — please confirm the exact balance in portal → Subscriptions → Free Trial, the API doesn't expose it). If so:

- **Remaining ≈ CAD $115** → at $21.7/day the credit dies **~July 9–10** — not "3 weeks from now." The subscription gets disabled at credit exhaustion, not at day 30.
- **Scale the harness down today** and burn drops to ~$4.3/day → the same $115 lasts **~26 days**, comfortably past the day-30 mark (~July 26). The W1–3 plan timeline survives without paying anything.

## Recommendation

Stop the four harness apps now (`az containerapp stop` on `dataq-harness-airflow`, `-airflow-worker`, `-airflow-trigger`, `-redis` — fully reversible with `start` for demo/E2E windows). Two caveats: DataQ's 10-min Airflow polling will log connection failures while it's down (possible #419 alert noise — snooze or disable the Airflow connection's suites), and the Flow B/C harness legs are dead until restarted. ADF ($0.27/day) can stay.

This also refines the earlier upgrade math: post-upgrade PAYG steady state is **~CAD $130/mo as-deployed, likely ~CAD $60–80 trimmed** (api scale-to-zero, worker 0.5 vCPU), with the harness woken only when needed.

Want me to stop the harness apps now and fold this (credit-runway note + harness-wake runbook) into #590/#591?
Yeah, stop the harness jobs and lets try to wrap it up by 30 days mark
On it — stopping the harness compute, quieting its ADF triggers, and locking the decision into the plan.

input.content:
---
name: project-azure-winddown
description: Azure free-trial wind-down — harness compute STOPPED 2026-07-04, no PAYG upgrade, hard wrap-up by day-30 (~2026-07-26); cost actuals + wake runbook on #590
metadata:
  type: project
---

Decision (2026-07-04): **no pay-as-you-go upgrade** — DataQ wraps up its Azure presence by
the free-trial day-30 mark (**~2026-07-26**; W3 milestone due 2026-07-25 is the hard
deadline for #590 teardown + #591 local-first posture). Snowflake subscription lapses
separately ~2026-07-05..10 (v1.1 W1, #588).

**Why:** Cost Management actuals (billing currency **CAD**): burn was ~21.7/day
(harness = 80% — Airflow scheduler 8.99 + 2×2-vCPU workers 6.92/day); CAD 144.80 of the
free credit already consumed by Jul 4 → credit would have died ~Jul 9–10. Postgres
B1ms/32GB is $0 (12-month free SKU). App-only burn ~CAD 4.5/day.

**State since 2026-07-04:** the 4 harness container apps (`dataq-harness-airflow`,

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# harness_window.sh — open/close an ad-hoc DataQ harness test window on Azure.
#
# The harness compute was stopped 2026-07-04 (cost wind-down, DataQ #590): while
# awake it burns ~CAD 17/day (~0.70/hour); stopped it costs ~0. This script wakes
# it, optionally kicks the data flows, and puts it back to sleep.
#
# Usage:
#   harness_window.sh status           # show app/trigger/job states (read-only)
#   harness_window.sh start            # wake: redis -> airflow -> worker -> trigger, ADF triggers on, jobs resumed
#   harness_window.sh run [--adf]      # kick the 5 mockdata jobs (+ optionally the Flow-A ADF pipelines*)
#   harness_window.sh stop             # sleep: triggers off, jobs suspended, apps stopped (reverse order)
#   harness_window.sh window [--adf]   # start -> run -> stop, one shot
#
# *--adf create-runs pl_flow_a_customers + pl_flow_a_orders. Flow A loads
#  SNOWFLAKE — after the Snowflake subscription lapses (v1.1 W1, #588) these runs
#  will fail; the mockdata jobs (file landing) keep working regardless.
#  Airflow DAGs are NOT triggered here — they fire on their own cron while the
#  scheduler is awake (or trigger manually in the Airflow UI).
set -euo pipefail

SUB="37e710fd-b956-4a2e-94f2-b132bb26b3d6"
RG="dataq-rg"
ADF="dataq-harness-adf"
ARM="https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/$RG/providers"
APP_API="2024-03-01"
JOB_API="2024-08-02-preview"
AIRFLOW_URL="https://dataq-harness-airflow.purplefield-f7322a1b.westus2.azurecontainerapps.io"

# Wake order matters: broker first, scheduler before workers. Stop is the reverse.
APPS_START=(dataq-harness-redis dataq-harness-airflow dataq-harness-airflow-worker dataq-harness-airflow-trigger)
APPS_STOP=(dataq-harness-airflow-trigger dataq-harness-airflow-worker dataq-harness-airflow dataq-harness-redis)
TRIGGERS=(tr_customers_daily tr_orders_landed)
JOBS=(mockdata-orders mockdata-inventory mockdata-tracking mockdata-feedback mockdata-supply)

log() { printf '%s %s\n' "$(date +%H:%M:%S)" "$*"; }

app_state() { az containerapp show -n "$1" -g "$RG" --query properties.runningStatus -o tsv 2>/dev/null; }

wait_app() { # wait_app <name> <wanted-state>
  local name=$1 want=$2 i
  for i in $(seq 1 60); do
    [[ "$(app_state "$name")" == "$want" ]] && { log "  $name -> $want"; return 0; }
    sleep 5
  done
  log "  TIMEOUT: $name never reached $want"; return 1
}

cmd_status() {
  az containerapp list -g "$RG" --query "[?starts_with(name,'dataq-harness')].{name:name, running:properties.runningStatus}" -o table
  az datafactory trigger list -g "$RG" --factory-name "$ADF" --query "[].{name:name, state:properties.runtimeState}" -o table
  local j
  for j in "${JOBS[@]}"; do
    printf '%s last execution: ' "$j"
    az rest --method GET --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/jobs/$j/executions?api-version=$JOB_API" -o json 2>/dev/null |
      python3 -c "import json,sys; v=json.load(sys.stdin)['value']; print(v[0]['properties']['status']+' @ '+v[0]['properties']['startTime'] if v else 'none')"
  done
}

cmd_start() {
  local app t j
  log "Waking harness (billing ~CAD 0.70/hour while awake)..."
  for app in "${APPS_START[@]}"; do
    az rest --method POST --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/containerApps/$app/start?api-version=$APP_API" -o none 2>/dev/null || true
    wait_app "$app" Running
    if [[ "$app" == "dataq-harness-airflow" ]]; then
      log "  waiting for Airflow /health..."
      for _ in $(seq 1 60); do
        curl -sf --max-time 10 "$AIRFLOW_URL/health" >/dev/null 2>&1 && { log "  Airflow healthy"; break; }
        sleep 10
      done
    fi
  done
  for t in "${TRIGGERS[@]}"; do
    az datafactory trigger start -g "$RG" --factory-name "$ADF" --name "$t" >/dev/null && log "  ADF trigger $t -> Started"
  done
  for j in "${JOBS[@]}"; do
    az rest --method POST --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/jobs/$j/resume?api-version=$JOB_API" -o none 2>/dev/null && log "  job $j resumed" || true
  done
  log "Harness is UP. Remember to '$0 stop' when done."
}

cmd_run() {
  local with_adf=${1:-} j execs=()
  log "Starting the 5 mockdata job executions (parallel)..."
  for j in "${JOBS[@]}"; do
    local ename
    ename=$(az rest --method POST --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/jobs/$j/start?api-version=$JOB_API" -o json 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get('name',''))")
    log "  $j -> execution ${ename:-<unknown>}"
    execs+=("$j:$ename")
  done
  log "Waiting for job executions to finish (timeout 35m each)..."
  local pair status
  for pair in "${execs[@]}"; do
    j=${pair%%:*}; local e=${pair#*:}
    [[ -z "$e" ]] && continue
    for _ in $(seq 1 70); do
      status=$(az rest --method GET --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/jobs/$j/executions/$e?api-version=$JOB_API" -o json 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['properties']['status'])" 2>/dev/null || echo Unknown)
      [[ "$status" != "Running" && "$status" != "Unknown" ]] && break
      sleep 30
    done
    log "  $j: $status"
  done
  if [[ "$with_adf" == "--adf" ]]; then
    log "Triggering Flow-A ADF pipelines (needs live Snowflake!)..."
    local p rid
    for p in pl_flow_a_customers pl_flow_a_orders; do
      rid=$(az datafactory pipeline create-run -g "$RG" --factory-name "$ADF" --name "$p" --query runId -o tsv)
      log "  $p -> run $rid"
      for _ in $(seq 1 60); do
        status=$(az datafactory pipeline-run show -g "$RG" --factory-name "$ADF" --run-id "$rid" --query status -o tsv)
        [[ "$status" != "InProgress" && "$status" != "Queued" ]] && break
        sleep 15
      done
      log "  $p: $status"
    done
  fi
  log "Run phase done. Airflow DAGs fire on their own cron while awake (or trigger in the UI: $AIRFLOW_URL)."
}

cmd_stop() {
  local t j app
  log "Putting the harness back to sleep..."
  for t in "${TRIGGERS[@]}"; do
    az datafactory trigger stop -g "$RG" --factory-name "$ADF" --name "$t" >/dev/null && log "  ADF trigger $t -> Stopped"
  done
  for j in "${JOBS[@]}"; do
    az rest --method POST --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/jobs/$j/suspend?api-version=$JOB_API" -o none 2>/dev/null && log "  job $j suspended" || true
  done
  for app in "${APPS_STOP[@]}"; do
    az rest --method POST --url "$ARM/Microsoft.App/containerApps/$app/stop?api-version=$APP_API" -o none 2>/dev/null || true
    wait_app "$app" Stopped
  done
  log "Harness is DOWN (burn back to ~CAD 4.5/day, app only)."
}

case "${1:-}" in
  status) cmd_status ;;
  start)  cmd_start ;;
  run)    cmd_run "${2:-}" ;;
  stop)   cmd_stop ;;
  window) cmd_start; cmd_run "${2:-}"; cmd_stop ;;
  *) grep '^#' "$0" | sed 's/^# \{0,1\}//' | head -20; exit 1 ;;
esac

Done. The script is at **`~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/scripts/harness_window.sh`** (harness-side, deliberately not in the repo per ADR 0021). For ad-hoc testing you just call:

```bash
~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/scripts/harness_window.sh window        # start → run flows → stop, one shot
~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/scripts/harness_window.sh window --adf  # same, + Flow-A ADF pipelines (needs live Snowflake)
```

Or the pieces individually: `status` (read-only), `start`, `run [--adf]`, `stop`.

What it does:

- **`start`** wakes things in dependency order — redis → Airflow scheduler (waits for `/health` to go green) → workers → trigger app — then starts both ADF triggers and resumes the 5 mockdata cron jobs. It reminds you it's billing (~CAD 0.70/hour while awake).
- **`run`** kicks all 5 mockdata job executions in parallel and polls each to completion (they land fresh files, which the ADF blob trigger picks up while awake). `--adf` additionally `create-run`s `pl_flow_a_customers` + `pl_flow_a_orders` and polls them — gated behind a flag because Flow A loads Snowflake, which dies this week (#588). Airflow DAGs aren't force-triggered; they fire on their own cron while the scheduler is awake (the script prints the UI URL if you want to trigger one manually).
- **`stop`** reverses everything: ADF triggers off, jobs suspended, apps stopped in reverse order, each verified `Stopped`.

Verification status, honestly: `bash -n` clean, and `status` ran against the live subscription — it correctly shows all four apps `Stopped`, both triggers `Stopped`, and the last (pre-suspension) job executions. The stop/suspend/trigger/executions ARM calls are the exact ones proven earlier today; the only paths not yet exercised are app `/start` and the Airflow health wait, since running them would wake the harness and spend credit. First real use — likely the W3 final live E2E, or your next ad-hoc test — is the true end-to-end validation; if `start` misbehaves I'd suspect only the health-wait timing, not the calls.

Also recorded: a pointer comment on [#590](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/590) (the script supersedes the manual runbook there) and the cross-session memory note now references the script path.
the schedule jobs will run as manual with this script right?

Mostly yes, with one exception worth spelling out — and one edge I can verify right now for a fraction of a cent.

- **Mockdata jobs (the 5 ACA cron jobs): yes.** `run` fires them via the on-demand `start` endpoint — they execute immediately as manual executions, no waiting for their cron. The cron itself is also resumed during a window, so if a job's scheduled time happens to land inside your window it can *additionally* fire once on schedule.
- **ADF (`--adf`): yes.** `create-run` is a manual pipeline run, bypassing the daily schedule trigger (which, in a short window, would likely never fire on its own). Note the blob-event trigger is live during the window, so `pl_flow_a_orders` may also fire automatically when the mockdata files land — you can see it twice.
- **Airflow DAGs: no — the exception.** The script doesn't force-trigger DAG runs; they only fire if their cron lands inside the window (or you trigger manually in the Airflow UI). I can add a `--dags` flag that POSTs to the Airflow REST API (`/api/v1/dags/{id}/dagRuns`) if you want full one-shot coverage — it needs the Airflow credential from Key Vault.

DataQ-side: manual runs are ingested exactly like scheduled ones (the 10-min poll picks up all runs regardless of how they were triggered), so trigger bindings still fire on success.

One edge to verify: whether a manual `start` works while a job is **suspended** (matters if someone calls `run` without `start` first). Let me test that now — one mockdata job, costs ~a cent, no downstream fires since the blob trigger is stopped.

cmd_dags() {
  local user pass d rid state
  user=$(terraform -chdir="$TF_DIR" output -raw airflow_admin_user)
  pass=$(terraform -chdir="$TF_DIR" output -raw airflow_admin_password)
  log "Triggering the cron DAGs via the Airflow REST API (sequential)..."
  for d in "${DAGS[@]}"; do
    # a dagRun created on a paused DAG sits queued forever -> unpause first (idempotent)
    curl -sf --max-time 30 -X PATCH "$AIRFLOW_URL/api/v1/dags/$d?update_mask=is_paused" \
      -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --user "$user:$pass" -d '{"is_paused": false}' >/dev/null
    rid=$(curl -sf --max-time 30 -X POST "$AIRFLOW_URL/api/v1/dags/$d/dagRuns" \
      -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --user "$user:$pass" -d '{}' |
      python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['dag_run_id'])")
    log "  $d -> $rid"
    for _ in $(seq 1 60); do
      state=$(curl -sf --max-time 30 --user "$user:$pass" \
        "$AIRFLOW_URL/api/v1/dags/$d/dagRuns/$rid" |
        python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['state'])" 2>/dev/null || echo unknown)
      [[ "$state" != "queued" && "$state" != "running" && "$state" != "unknown" ]] && break
      sleep 15
    done
    log "  $d: $state"
  done
}

!/usr/bin/env bash
harness_window.sh — open/close an ad-hoc DataQ harness test window on Azure.

The harness compute was stopped 2026-07-04 (cost wind-down, DataQ #590): while

content.text:
Done — here's what the Airflow docs check found and what changed in the script.

**Docs findings (Airflow 2.10.5, the harness's exact version):**
- The stable REST API is the right surface: **`POST /api/v1/dags/{dag_id}/dagRuns`** with an empty JSON body triggers a manual run; **basic auth** works because the harness sets `REDACTED,session` (the TF comment says it's kept exactly for DataQ's poller). The admin user clears the required role (POST dagRuns needs ≥User).
- One documented gotcha worth the check: **a run created on a paused DAG never executes** — it just queues. So the script PATCHes `is_paused: false` (with `update_mask`) before triggering, idempotently.
- Run status polls `GET /api/v1/dags/{id}/dagRuns/{run_id}` until it leaves `queued`/`running`.

**Script updates** (`harness_window.sh`, syntax-checked):
- New **`--dags` flag** on `run`/`window`: unpauses + manually triggers the three cron DAGs sequentially (`flow_a_snowflake_load` → `flow_a_uc_reference` → `flow_b_medallion`, mirroring their cron ordering) and reports each terminal state. Credentials come from the harness Terraform outputs (`airflow_admin_user`/`_password` — verified they resolve locally, no Key Vault dependency).
- `flow_a_payments_event` is deliberately **not** force-triggered: it's `schedule=None` and expects an Event-Grid `conf['blob_url']` payload — it fires naturally when the mockdata jobs land files while the harness is awake. content.text:
`low effort → 1 diff pass → no verify → ≤4 findings`

## Turn 1 — read

One tool call: read the unified diff (`git diff @{upstream}...HEAD; git diff HEAD`
to cover both committed and uncommitted changes, or `git diff main...HEAD` /
the target passed as an argument). Skip test/fixture
hunks (`test/`, `spec/`, `__tests__/`, `*_test.*`, `*.test.*`,
`fixtures/`, `testdata/`) — test-file changes are not reviewed at this level.
No subagents, no full-file reads.

## Turn 2 — findings

Flag runtime-correctness bugs visible from the hunk alone: inverted/wrong
condition, off-by-one, null/undefined deref where adjacent lines show the value
can be absent, removed guard, falsy-zero check, missing `await`,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in a catch that should propagate.
Also flag — still from the hunk alone — new code that duplicates an existing
helper visible in the diff context, and dead code the diff leaves behind.

Do **not** flag style, naming, perf, missing tests, or anything outside the
hunk.

Output at most **4 findings**, most-severe first, one line each:
`path/to/file.ext:123 — what's wrong and the concrete failure`. If nothing
qualifies, output exactly `(none)`.

Status while CI runs on [#599](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/599):

- **Project audit done:** the only stale pre-v1 items were **#147** and **#122** — both actually closed back in Week 5 (#147 via the W5 closeout merge, #122 by #297/#298), so I flipped their project status to Done. **#124** (DQ-dimension classification) is genuinely open and already properly migrated to post-v1 (Backlog milestone, Theme 2 in the roadmap doc) — it stays Todo. The project's non-Done list is now exactly the 27 scheduled v1.1 issues.
- **CLAUDE.md note:** PR #599 adds a §12 row for `harness_window.sh` — including the "harness is stopped by default, this is how you wake it" context so future sessions don't misread the stopped apps. The review pass caught one factual overstatement (only the `flow_a_snowflake_load` DAG needs Snowflake, not all `--dags` targets), fixed via a `fixup:` commit.

I'll merge #599 once CI is green (per your earlier green-light pattern — say so if you'd rather merge it yourself).
